Alright guys, well welcome to Theme Plate 2, The Bieber. I mean how can we not have it, of course created by two Canadians. I'm not saying we are Bieber lovers, we did make a radio head reference just before, so don't question our musical tastes too early in the game. But what can we borrow from Bieber when it comes to writing email copy? Turns out not a lot, but we are going to take the name Sorry. Sorry is a very powerful word. I mean think about some examples of this argument between partners and finally someone says sorry and then the other says that's all I needed you to say, that's all I needed to hear. And all of a sudden all this fighting finally ends, they put those differences in pride aside and become resourceful. So an apology goes a long way. It creates that resourcefulness and renewed optimism we're looking for and it absolves the other person of any feelings or doubts that they were the ones at fault. That's what we want to activate here. We want to clear the past so that our prospect can get more present and excited about where we're taking them and as a side note when you apologize on behalf of other things, people, paradigm oppressors, competitors in your industry, it subtly positions you as a trusted and benevolent authority. Like you need to be a pretty high up authority to apologize on behalf of someone else. What if like, it's kind of like how parents will apologize on behalf of their kids. That puts them at that position of authority and it's the same thing when you apologize on behalf of your industry or competitors who just couldn't know better because they're not at that same level as you or as benevolent as you. So that's what we're going for here. That's the vibe. This is the theme plate. So we're going to start off with the apology. Just flat out. That gets attention. What is this person sorry for? Then we'll get into that moment of highest tension. We're going to get into the ways they've tried to solve it. We'll show that it wasn't their fault and then we'll offer that way out that better way, that more resourceful way that comes at the other end of an apology and then we'll have that call to action and we'll qualify them in that exclusively empowered way. So we're really using a lot of the same elements we've already written about and explained throughout this module. We're just using a different type of container. This is going to be useful as either a second email to send if they didn't opt in from that first exclusively empowered call out. This will just give them kind of that release, that release from blame in the past and that resourcefulness that this time it can work. So that's what we are aiming for here. So let's just go through an example and this is also from our 10X launches launch. So you may have seen this. You may not have. This is something that you could send to people when they didn't sign up for that first email. So if you signed up from the first call out, you probably wouldn't have gotten this one. So let's get into it. It's a subject line. Sorry. I'm sorry. That hell of an open loop. One word, five letters, but just one massive open loop. What is this person apologizing for? So I'm sorry, Ryan. I'm sorry that the online course you just paid 5K for didn't warn you about how hard it would be to execute all those tiny email icons on the funnel mat. That the success of your funnel and heck, your entire business fully hinges on writing those little things and writing them fast. That an expertly curated concoction of binaural beats, bulletproof coffee and brain drugs with bad names haven't been enough to help you blast through it. And I'm sorry that despite your best efforts to do it yourself, you've been struck down by confusing over complicated and impractical systems written by old, mostly dead copywriters who didn't have to contend with unceasing Slack notifications, impossible deadlines and more impossible screaming six year old. So this is once again, all that stuff that's part of that moment of highest tension and being super specific, you know, down to bulletproof coffee and brain drugs and Slack notifications and screaming six year olds like these are all real things that are prospect and maybe you as someone who is in this course experiences day by day. So it's fully grounded in that reality. So I'm really, really sorry. And on behalf of all these things that have put you in this perilous place with the success of your launch dangling in the balance, please accept my special invitation to make it up to you. So this is where we're framing our call to action as that apology as that making up for all these things that didn't work or that just haven't been serving them. So that's another way to frame your call to action. Starting next week, Joe and I will be hosting the launch and profit workshops. This is a must attend if you're and this is where we're going to get into those exclusive empowered points, which you saw before party get started next Tuesday, click here to stay within blast radius for when the copy bombs start falling and then our sign off. And then like you saw before, we could do those. We could just bring in a little more curiosity and sell the event more and, you know, and with another call to action on, don't forget to grab your seat. But the main elements here are really quite simply apologizing on behalf of your industry, apologizing on behalf of their situation and, you know, just those oppressors and by apologizing, you're really positioning yourself as the authority, the authority who's capable of apologizing on behalf of something and offering a better alternative. So that is the Bieber sorry for using something called the Bieber sorry, not sorry. I'm going to stop saying sorry now because it's the most Canadian thing ever. So that is it. Use this theme plate. If you did not get the opt-in or the click with the plate number one, this is a really great follow up for that. So that's it. We got another one coming up. So I will see you in the next walkthrough.
